speaker: interviewer
text: Any time, Berm.

speaker: interviewer
text: April 21, 1996-- interview with Faye Merin Porter; interviewer, Lynn Bryce; in Saint Louis Park, Minnesota, United States. The language of the interview is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Shlomo Sender Porter. I live in Baltimore. I was born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. My wife, Shoshana Raizel, is not with me. She is with our children in Philadelphia. My oldest daughter is Esther Malka. And my next daughter is Elka Chava, who is named after one of my mother's sisters. Then my oldest son is Yisroel, who is named after my father. And then my next son is Nosson Nota Porter, who is named after my mother's father. And my youngest son is Mayer Porter.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to introduce other parts of our-- our family-- my sister, Yenta Bella Porter Smith, and her husband, Rabbi Mayer Smith; their son, Avram Zvi. Avram is my mother's father-- named after my mother's father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather. The little young lady is [NON-ENGLISH] Chaya Malka. And Chaya was named after my sister who was killed during the Holocaust. Next to her is Aryeh Nosson. Aryeh was named after Rabbi Aryeh Kaplan, and Nosson was named after my grandfather, also my mother's father. Next to him is Yisroel [NON-ENGLISH], who's also named after Yisroel, after my father; and Shragi [NON-ENGLISH] Smith. The [NON-ENGLISH] was named after my father's uncle who's-- who died in Europe; and my older brother, Jack Nosson Porter, who is named after both of his grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I would like to mention, you know, my wife, Miriam [NON-ENGLISH] Porter. She couldn't be here. We live in Newton Highlands, Massachusetts, and we grew up in Milwaukee. She-- both her parents are survivors of the [NON-ENGLISH] family. And we have two children, Gabrielle and Danielle, and they are not-- [LAUGHS] --named after anyone in particular. And-- well--

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I-- I just wanted to say-- maybe I'll say just a few words as-- I-- I only wish that our father, my father, was not-- is not here, Irving Porter [NON-ENGLISH], a partisan. You know, you'll see pictures of him, you know, later. But he was a wonderful man, and of course, a fighter for the Jewish people and for all mankind, and a great partner to-- to my mother, our mother. And my mother is a very, very special person. And we are all very proud to-- to be part of this family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aryeh? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How could I say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to say that my bubbe is really special to me, because she always is taking care of me and-- whenever I'm sick. And she always tells me interesting stories about how-- how she survived in the Holocaust and other people. So she's very special to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love my bubbe. She's one of the-- she's one of the unique-- unique women in my life. And she's very special. And she's a part of my heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say that growing up in my parents' home, my parents being survivors, you might think that it was a-- it might be a sad home to grow up in. But my parents instilled in us a quality of purpose and happiness. And they saw the good in all people. And they saw that though their life was very difficult during the war, they came away feeling that there is still good in mankind and that it's good to be a Jew. And it's joyful to be a Jew. And that we all growing up in the home felt a lot of joy, a lot of love, and we also felt that there was a reason and there was a purpose for-- for us to be able to continue a-- a Jewish family after so many of people in our family were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we all grew up with a purpose. And that made our growing up very special and very unique. And I-- and I thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our-- our home was a religious home. And our parents both felt that if they would drop their [NON-ENGLISH], and their observance of Shabbos, kashruth, like many other Holocaust survivors did, they would have given a posthumous victory to Hitler. And therefore, they continued with great joy and self sacrifice to make sure that the children should be sent to Jewish day schools. If-- when there was a day school, my sister was taken out of public school and put back one grade in order to-- when a day school was founded in Milwaukee, in order that-- that he would give the best day school education-- Jewish education to the children. And when it came time to-- after my brother's bar mitzvah, they encouraged that he should go on in this learning, go to Israel. And when I made a decision to go to Yeshiva, they encouraged it also. And even though it meant changes in the-- in the entire family life, they-- they went along with everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to say about my parents, even though they were-- they were partisans, they were fighters, which was also rare, and-- yet I believe that it was not just the Holocaust that we have to remember. I mean, what is the basis of our-- the Jewish religion? It's culture. It's history. It's sedekah. It's good deeds. It's righteous deeds and not dwelling on the death and destruction, basically. I think that's something that we dwell too much, perhaps, on destruction rather than construction and on culture, and on family, and continuity. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Although I'm not a part of the immediate family, I married into the family. I've learned a great deal from my mother-in-law and from my father-in-law, who I had just a brief opportunity to-- to know and to-- to get to-- to meet prior to his passing-- a very unique and special family-- that our children are all named after people who were from Europe, who are part of the history of the Jewish people-- are now part of the present of the Jewish people. And we hope with what we're doing with our children, they will be the future of the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Lynn Bryce, spelled L-Y-N-N B-R-Y-C-E. Today's date is April 21, 1996. It is my honor and privilege to conduct this interview with Faygeh Porter, in Saint Louis Park, Minnesota.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say and spell your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say and spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Faygeh Porter-Arenzon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Faye, F-A-Y-E. Porter is P-O-R-T-E-R; Arenzon, A-R-E-N-Z-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horodok, Volhynia gubernia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horodok is H-O-R-O-D-O-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: The country is-- I'll tell you. When I was born, it was Russia-- in 19-- in 1910. In that time I was four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Russian till 19-- till 1939. No, no, no-- better-- after the first world war, the Polish government was till 1939. Then the Russian came. 1939 the communist government came to us, and they stayed till not long. And the-- and the Nazis came.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Nazis came in 1940-- or better, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what large city is Horodok near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Horodok is-- was Maniewicze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maniewicze.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maniewicze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the Jewish community in which you grew up? It's--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was come-- about 500 people-- 500 people-- not-- it a small shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: A shtetl.

speaker: interviewee
text: A shtetl. And-- and it was a quiet. We-- you know-- a quiet life. My father was a-- a Talmudic teacher, Hebrew, Talmudic, and-- until he-- till-- you know, the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the family support itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my father was teaching.

speaker: interviewer
text: Teaching.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was teacher. He got a salary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did your family belong to any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, no, no organization, just strict orthodox. They knew to keep the Shabbos holy, and holidays, and-- and tradition. You know, it's strict orthodox. My family was strict orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family was an Hasidic orthodox family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father was a Hasidic-- Stoliner-- Stoliner-- Stoliner Hasidic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stoliner Hasidic. Did your father or your mother follow a particular Hasidic rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Stoliner rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Stoliner rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stolin is a city. The name Stolin, and they called this Stoliner Hasidim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandparents, were they also Hasidim?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you related to any of the prominent Hasidic Tzadikim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now? Right now I-- sometime the-- the Stoliner Hasidim, they come to collect money, you know, for the-- for this group, for the Hasidim. And that's all, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father travel to Stoliner to the-- to the rabbi there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime. And sometime the rabbi comes to our shtetl, yeah, once in a while-- once in a-- one year-- once a year or once in two years-- that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did your family seek advice from the rabbi that you were aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask him for advice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. They ask him advice. They ask him for a-- for pray-- to pray for us. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he speak to you in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: To me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were too young-- too young.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was too young. I wanted to tell you one-- what's happened. One rabbi came. And my son, Jack, was selected to the-- to the-- war-- the war, what was here in America, the-- oye! You know? Oh! I forgot. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: The draft.

speaker: interviewer
text: The draft?

speaker: interviewee
text: The draft.

speaker: interviewer
text: Draft.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so when one rabbi gave him a prayer, in the-- he didn't-- they-- they didn't select him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the Hasidic literature you read in your home before the war? Do you have any memory of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have a library. We read a lot of books-- Yiddish-- Yiddish books. And-- and it was a fine, fine place, a quiet place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the rabbi after the German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: After the-- after the-- after the German occupation? What happened to the rabbi? I think he died before. He died before that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the relationship between Jews and non-Jews in-- in the shtetl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was all kind of non-Jewish. You know. Some are very good. Some are-- so it was nice. We lived in a-- in a-- in a shtetl with a lot of Ukraine people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a Hebrew school, or did you go to a regular public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a public school. And I was taught by-- by my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-Semitism in the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No,

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were growing up, did your family apprentice you? Did you have any trade? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Trade, I learned how to-- sewing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sewing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went for-- for three months to a different city to take a sewing class, you know, a course in sewing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were looking forward to-- to growing up in the shtetl, what were your goals? How did you see your future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I-- I'll-- I'll tell you. Mine future was in the same-- I met my husband, the next door neighbor, and was very young. I remember one thing-- when I was 17 years old. Through a matchmaker, they came to us from the other city to-- their-- the boy came, and the matchmaker, and his mother. You know? Can you imagine what-- what-- what kind of life it was-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Europe? So nothing happened. I didn't like that guy. Did-- could you describe your family life? What was it like-- the holidays, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Holidays-- strict, strict Pesach, you know. And it was special, special, altogether the family, with tradition, the kashrus in-- in-- in the-- it was-- it was a life was never be forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of the families in the shtetl Hasidim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them are-- were Hasidim, some not. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember 1939 and the-- the beginnings of war? What was the reaction in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, the German started after Poland-- on Poland. And in 1939, in the Polacks, they couldn't resist the Germans. So they-- the army split. And part of the army went through toward Horodok, in through Maniewicze. And the-- and the communist-- the communists, they looking for them. They looking for the Polish army. And they send up two airplanes. And they threw leaflets-- the two-- the two airplanes. They threw leaflets to surrender. And the-- and the-- and the Polish commander give an order to-- through a [NON-ENGLISH] to fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the two airplanes flew away. And after a 1/2 hour, they came maybe 20 airplanes on this shtetl. And they started to throw fire bombs. In Horodok?

speaker: interviewee
text: And all-- this was already in Maniewicze. And the all-- all shtetl was on fire. They kill a lot of Polish, the part of the army. And we just run after the-- through the windows to be alive. And we lost everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was on fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family-- can we back up just a little bit? You were in Horodok, and you-- you were a family of how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was from eight children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight children. And how many brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four brothers and then three sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now how did you-- at what point did the family move, or were they forced to move to-- from Horodok to Maniewicze?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well some of them, they got married. And-- and-- you know-- and-- and they-- and we moved. You know, the war started. And the government sent out everybody. Because they were supposed to go through. And they send us to Poland, Kielce gubernia.

speaker: interviewer
text: They sent you first to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, everybody. They sent out the Ukraine people, the Jewish people. They send out, because the war is supposed to go through our region.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through Horodok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first you went to-- where did you go immediately when you were asked?

speaker: interviewee
text: They send us out. They took us on trains, and then-- and we went to Poland, deep Poland-- Kielce gubernia.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Kielce.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Kielce gubernia, that means the state of Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1914. Wait a minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: 1914, All right. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: --this-- this was before World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when-- let's think for a moment about World War II, when you were living in Horodok.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were living in-- we're living already in Maniewicze.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- all right. Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Maniewicze.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your family was moved to Maniewicze. Were you married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have children at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- how many children did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two-- two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two? And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name is Pesel and Chaya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two little girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two little girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were in Maniewicze.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Maniewicze.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were you yet in a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a short time in a ghetto. And-- they didn't-- we didn't have a-- too long-- a long time a ghetto. No-- very short, the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Maniewicze.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, they took all the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the first action [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the-- yeah. And the-- and they said that they'll take them to work. They didn't take them to work. They right taking them to the graves in Maniewicze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that to a field outside Maniewicze?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that field?

speaker: interviewee
text: That field is the-- Shlomo, maybe you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the [NON-ENGLISH]-- maybe the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the horse's field?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The horse-- the horse [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Horse-- the horse's-- horse's grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: The horse's grave

speaker: interviewer
text: Horse's grave. And would you-- would you say that again, in the-- the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] .

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] .

speaker: interviewee
text: Horse is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Horse is [NON-ENGLISH]. And they shot the men in the field. Which family members were shot at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My two brothers, my father-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your two-- your two brothers and the-- your father-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Father-in-law-- my father-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the women-- the women were-- were-- were spared at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we live in fear til 1942. And before 1942--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was it like for you, and your sisters, and your mother, when-- when the men were taken in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you imagine? That was a-- a big tragedy. You know. We cannot go out-- the Nazi occupation. You cannot go in the-- in the middle of the street. You-- you were worse than a ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any rabbi there? Was there any rabbi there with you at that point for the community to gather around?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did your family actively seek the blessing of the rabbi at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to the rabbi for-- for prayer--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi, he said, run to-- run to this country, Russia. There you will be survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that sustain you? Did it help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody believe it-- what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So nobody ran?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them--

speaker: interviewer
text: But--

speaker: interviewee
text: --very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did--

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my two cousins, they went to Russia and there. And they came back alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now your husband was not taken at that first-- in 1949.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Mine-- my husband, when they hear that's they're going to take the men to work, he says, I'm not going to wait for them to go to work. And he-- he-- he went to a-- a farm-- to a farmer, to a village, a farmer village, and there survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what-- then what happened to your husband then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then what happened, my husband, he came back. After that he came back. And-- no. He made up the-- the mayor of this Horodok, he made up a sign. A lot of people give a signature that you can take me over to this village. And after that, in 1942, the-- the Nazis came with the truck and they-- there was only three families. And they took us all on that truck, and they put us in Maniewicze in a jail. And the-- some of the-- some, they had-- of this Maniewicze people-- they find out that we are in jail, and they came, and they-- and they free us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people from Maniewicze freed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they-- this was a Judenrat. You know what a Judenrat?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, the Judenrat from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Judenrat-- and they came and they get us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they free you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because at that time, it was not the order to kill from the-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. It was not order to kill. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they freed you, you returned to the Maniewicze ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Maniewicze ghetto, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your-- your mother and sisters. Who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and sisters, and mine mother-in-law. And a lot of-- a lot of people got, you know, in a ghetto, they-- we live in one house-- you can count two, three families in one house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any dreams while you were in this situation in the ghetto-- any dreams? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We dream that it could happen a miracle. That was our dream. And that-- that's all. We-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what were you doing-- with-- how old were the little-- were your little girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years and two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four years and two years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But your mother and three sisters, what-- was your mother-in-law with you then also?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-in-law, mine-- mine parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was 19-- what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop.
